text: This is a heads up that this video contains footage from surgery so you will be able to see certain organs and blood so do not watch if you're under 18.
So I thought I'd do a quick video demonstrating what actually happens to pieces of the body after they've been resected in surgery.
So this gentleman had a colorectal cancer which had metastasized to his lungs.
This was a dual operation where there was a lower lobe wedge resection and an upper lobe segmentectomy.
The segmentectomy is actually a bit of a bigger operation.

So both pieces of lung have been stuffed into that white specimen bag and the specimen bag can be drawn really tightly and it's made of a thick fibre so it doesn't burst and you actually have to pull it through the chest wall.
This is a robotic operation so the biggest port you actually have into the chest is about 10mm so it's quite a small margin to pull a big piece of lung through.
Once you pull the lung through you actually lose your CO2 seal so the chest will deflate after that so you have to do it at the end of the operation.
